includedSources.name: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: TSMC beat by 77% and the stock still fell — DeepSeek is why nobody bought the dip
items.idea: TSMC delivered 77% YoY earnings growth and investors sold anyway, while MSCI Asia Pacific fell 2.7%, the Nikkei dropped over 5%, Kioxia sank as much as 16% and TSMC lost roughly 4%. Meanwhile DeepSeek raised $7.4B at a $50B+ valuation to undercut Western model pricing, on the thesis that AI value migrates from scarce model providers toward power, infrastructure and low-cost distribution as intelligence becomes closer to a utility.
items.angle: Read together with MacroEdge's data-center-slowdown item below: both point at the same rotation — away from model-layer premium margins, toward power and infrastructure.
items.tweetA: TSMC just posted 77% earnings growth and the stock fell anyway. When beating by that much can't move the tape, positioning has replaced fundamentals as the thing being priced.
items.tweetB: DeepSeek raised $7.4B to make intelligence cheap. If that thesis is right, the AI trade doesn't die, it just stops paying model providers and starts paying whoever owns the power plant next to the datacenter.

charts.desc: No embedded chart image, but the piece references MSCI Asia Pacific, Nikkei and TSMC intraday moves — a multi-panel Asia session chart is likely in the full post. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Iran-US war has “transitioned” to a bilateral fight, and the Nasdaq's diamond top is confirming it
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson says the conflict has shifted from an Israel-Iran war to one “almost exclusively between the US and Iran,” with infrastructure strikes resuming in the past eight hours. Technically, the Nasdaq is trading below the ‘diamond top’ pattern flagged by his ‘Motherlode’ signal in early June (4 instances since 1990), and he expects a possible ‘war climax’ by Sunday if escalation continues into the weekend.
items.angle: Geopolitics and technicals converging on the same timeline is the tell — pairs with the Daily News Wall item on the stale June CPI print below.
items.tweetA: A technician is calling this a 4-times-since-1990 pattern (diamond top off a 'Motherlode' signal) at the exact moment a macro strategist says the Iran war just went bilateral, US-vs-Iran only. When the chart and the news cycle agree, that's usually the moment to actually worry.
items.tweetB: Everyone's still framing this as an Israel-Iran story. MacroEdge says that framing is already stale, it's the US and Iran now, direct, and the market hasn't finished repricing that shift.

charts.desc: Two charts referenced: the Nasdaq diamond-top pattern since the June 'Motherlode' signal, and the Kospi parabolic uptrend during Korea's market holiday. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Data center spend is 'sitting on the verge of a major slowdown' — and lenders are the tell, not hyperscalers
items.idea: Johnson argues data-center and AI capex, currently the engine of the US economy, is approaching a Q4 slowdown, and that “lenders are growing much more wary that data centers are ever going to have any payback (they won't).” MacroEdge is publishing its second AI/data-center bubble report next Wednesday, covering construction status, moratoriums, AI debt risk and the Oracle-OpenAI financing arrangement.
items.angle: Credit, not equity, is the leading indicator here — lender skepticism moves in weeks, equity sentiment can stay hopeful for quarters.
items.tweetA: Forget the equity tape for a second. The line that matters in this note is 'lenders are growing much more wary that data centers are ever going to have any payback.' Equity investors can stay hopeful for quarters. Lenders pull term sheets in weeks.
items.tweetB: The AI capex debate has been fought entirely on equity multiples all year. The actual tell is going to show up in credit spreads and covenants on data-center financing, Oracle-OpenAI is the one to watch, not the Nasdaq.

charts.desc: No chart in this teaser; full bubble report drops next Wednesday. Worth tracking.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: Korea and Taiwan were the cleanest expression of the AI trade — now they're the cleanest expression of its unwind
items.idea: Bloomberg's David Savage frames it plainly: the momentum trade that powered Asian equities through H1 2026 is reversing. Foreign selling has left Korean equities dependent on retail investors and leveraged ETFs, and SK Hynix-linked products can turn falling prices into forced selling as daily exposure rebalances. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) is down more than 18% from its June highs.
items.angle: The leveraged-ETF daily-rebalancing mechanic is the actual transmission channel, not sentiment — it forces more selling tomorrow regardless of fundamentals.
items.tweetA: SOX -18% from June highs isn't the scary number. The scary number is that Korean leveraged ETFs rebalance daily, which means falling SK Hynix prices mechanically force more selling tomorrow regardless of what the fundamentals do.
items.tweetB: Korea and Taiwan were the purest way to express the AI trade on the way up because retail and leverage piled in fastest. That's exactly why they're now the purest way to express the unwind, the same mechanism runs in both directions.

charts.desc: No chart in email; article likely carries a SOX-vs-Kospi relative chart. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: The EU just ordered Google to let rival AI assistants answer ‘Hey Google’ on Android
items.idea: Brussels' new DMA rulings require Google to give rival AI assistants the same system-level access as Gemini on Android, including voice-command triggers and hardware access, by July 2027, and to share Search-generated data with rival AI chatbots by January 2027. Non-compliance risks fines up to 10% of global annual turnover. Apple has already cited the DMA as its reason for withholding Siri AI from Europe entirely.
items.angle: This is a regulatory-arbitrage/balkanization story for AI distribution, not just an antitrust headline.
items.tweetA: Starting in 2027, Android users in the EU could set ChatGPT, Claude or Perplexity as their default 'Hey Google' assistant with full hardware access. That's the EU legislating away Google's single biggest AI distribution moat, on a timeline you can put on a calendar.
items.tweetB: Apple already used the DMA as its excuse to just not ship Siri AI in Europe at all. If Google's practical response to forced interoperability looks similar, the AI Tech Wave is heading toward a genuinely balkanized product experience, US/China won't be the only two flavors of AI.

charts.desc: Text/policy piece, no chart images. Worth opening for the full DMA compliance timeline.

items.title: McKinsey's pitch: govern AI agents like labor and capital, or the ROI math won't work
items.idea: McKinsey partners argue that as agentic AI spending climbs, the leaders who “govern machine work as thoughtfully as human labor, capital, and operations” will build lasting competitive advantage, implicitly conceding that most agentic AI deployments today run with weaker governance than a company would apply to a new hire or a capex project.
items.angle: Governance-as-moat is a useful counterweight to pure compute/model-scale narratives dominating the AI-infra conversation this week.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's quiet admission here: most companies are governing AI agents with less rigor than they'd apply to hiring an intern. The firms that fix that, treating machine work like labor and capital instead of a software subscription, are the ones McKinsey thinks actually capture the ROI.
items.tweetB: Everyone's arguing whether agentic AI spend is justified. McKinsey's answer isn't 'spend more' or 'spend less,' it's 'govern it like a P&L line.' That's a much harder sell to boards than either extreme, which is probably why almost nobody's doing it yet.

charts.desc: Newsletter digest blurb linking to a full report, no chart in the email itself. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: McKinsey's four-part fix for a problem nobody's pricing: who trains junior employees once AI does the entry-level work
items.idea: McKinsey's Bryan Hancock and Charlotte Seiler outline a four-part approach to helping early-career employees build judgment and problem-solving skills alongside AI, arguing the traditional apprenticeship model, where juniors learn by doing the grunt work senior staff no longer have time for, is breaking down as AI absorbs that same grunt work.
items.angle: A labor/education pipeline problem that predates AI headlines by decades but is now accelerating, worth flagging next to any AI-labor-displacement thesis, since the risk here is skill-formation, not headcount.
items.tweetA: The AI jobs debate is all about headcount. The quieter problem McKinsey is flagging is skill formation, junior employees used to learn by doing the exact grunt work AI now does. If nobody redesigns the apprenticeship model, you don't lose jobs, you lose the next generation of people who know how to do the senior job.
items.tweetB: Every AI-productivity story assumes today's experts stay experts. Nobody's modeling what happens in 15 years when the people who were supposed to become today's experts never did the reps.

charts.desc: Newsletter digest blurb, no chart in the email. Charts inside — worth opening the full report.

items.title: Noah Smith's takedown of Acemoglu: garment workers' wages tripled using the exact automation tech he says immiserates labor
items.idea: Reposting his 2024 review of Acemoglu & Johnson's ‘Power and Progress,’ Smith argues the book's central policy claim, that societies must ‘steer’ technology toward augmenting rather than replacing workers, collapses under its own historical examples: China's garment industry used the same automated textile technology that supposedly ‘immiserated’ 1700s British workers, and Chinese wages skyrocketed as apparel became its top export category; Bangladeshi incomes have tripled since 1990 running the identical unsteered playbook.
items.angle: Relevant right now because Acemoglu's 'steering' language just got written into the 'We Must Act Now' AI-safety statement, this is the intellectual fault line behind current AI labor-policy debates.
items.tweetA: Acemoglu's fix for AI-driven job losses is to 'steer' innovation toward augmenting workers, not replacing them. Noah Smith's rebuttal: Bangladesh ran the un-steered version of that exact playbook with 1700s-vintage British textile automation, and incomes there have tripled since 1990. The steering thesis doesn't survive contact with the data Acemoglu himself cites.
items.tweetB: Worth remembering as AI-safety statements start baking in 'steering' language: the same economist whose historical case studies get dismantled by his own examples is currently shaping how the econ profession thinks about regulating AI's labor impact.

charts.desc: Long-form text review, no charts. Worth opening in full for the historical case-by-case rebuttal.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom (via Bloomberg's Simon White)
items.title: Miran wants the Fed to watch M2 again — one strategist says the real signal is savings deposits, not headline money supply
items.idea: Bloomberg's Simon White argues headline M2 is too blunt for the Fed's inflation framework, but that falling 'defensive' savings-deposit balances, cash sitting idle rather than being deployed, have historically led inflation by roughly six to nine months. That framework currently points toward stickier inflation, tighter policy, and further upside risk for yields, just as Stephen Miran's new paper urges the Fed to bring money back into its forecasting toolkit.
items.angle: A concrete, dated (6-9 month lead) alternative inflation signal that cuts directly against the market currently trading June's soft CPI/PPI prints as the new base case.
items.tweetA: The Fed's last brush with M2 as a forecasting tool ended with them missing 2021 inflation entirely. White's fix isn't 'watch M2 again,' it's 'watch how fast savings deposits are draining,' a narrower signal with a 6-9 month lead time that's currently flashing stickier inflation, not disinflation.
items.tweetB: If savings-deposit depletion really leads inflation by 6-9 months, and the Fed ends up adopting this framework off Miran's paper, that's a policy-relevant signal arriving right as everyone else is trading June's soft prints as the new consensus.

charts.desc: Teaser links to a chart-based full article not captured in the email body. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: June's soft CPI is already stale, it printed before this week's Iran strikes shut Hormuz again
items.idea: June CPI fell 0.4% MoM (the biggest drop since April 2020, 3.5% YoY) and PPI fell 0.3% MoM, but both prints captured a month when Iran tensions had eased and energy fell. This week brought a fresh US-Iran re-escalation with Hormuz effectively shut and Trump reportedly foreshadowing an Iran blockade, meaning July's CPI, not June's, will be the print that actually reflects the war premium.
items.angle: A backward-looking-data trap: markets rallied on stale disinflation numbers right as the underlying driver (energy, via Hormuz) reversed.
items.tweetA: June CPI dropped the most since April 2020. Great headline, wrong month, Iran tensions had already eased by the time that data was collected. This week's re-escalation and a shut Hormuz will show up in July's print, not June's. The market is trading the wrong month's war premium.
items.tweetB: FOMC hike odds for July 29 collapsed from above 40% to 10-13% on the back of soft June inflation. That repricing happened the same week Trump foreshadowed an Iran blockade. Somebody's wrong about the September meeting, and it's probably the market that priced the last war, not this one.

charts.desc: Emoji-formatted news-wall digest, no chart images.

items.title: Retail sales ex-autos missed by 2x, but the Beige Book says price growth is flat or slowing in every single Fed district
items.idea: June retail sales ex-autos fell 0.2% against expectations of just a 0.1% decline, while the Beige Book confirmed price growth held flat or slowed across all twelve Fed districts and initial jobless claims fell to 208,000. The combination reads as disinflation without a growth scare: soft consumer, firm labor market, cooling prices everywhere at once.
items.angle: A textbook soft-landing data cluster, worth flagging precisely because it's about to collide with the Iran-driven July inflation print in the item above.
items.tweetA: All twelve Fed districts reporting flat-or-slowing price growth in the same Beige Book is a cleaner disinflation signal than any single CPI print. Pair it with claims at 208K and you have the textbook soft landing, for one more month, before July's Hormuz-driven energy shock hits the data.
items.tweetB: Retail sales ex-autos missed by double the expected decline (-0.2% vs -0.1%) and nobody noticed because the headline print was in line. The consumer is cooling faster than the number everyone's quoting suggests.

charts.desc: Text digest, no chart images.

items.title: The Nasdaq's -0.8% headline is hiding a much uglier chip-sector session
items.idea: TSMC's higher capex guidance spooked markets even as the company beat estimates, reviving AI-capex-ROI doubt. The Nasdaq's modest 0.8% decline masks far heavier damage concentrated specifically in semiconductor, memory and hardware names ('AI lifts while chips stumble,' per Saxo), and the Dow Jones Thematic AI Momentum Index is headed for its worst month since December 2023.
items.angle: Same TSMC data point as the Asia Wrap item above but a different trigger, capex-guide-as-catalyst rather than earnings-beat-ignored, three separate sources converging on the same underlying stress this week.
items.tweetA: TSMC beat and raised capex guidance. The market's read wasn't 'great quarter,' it was 'how is this ever going to earn a return.' Same data, completely different reads landing in three different inboxes this week.
items.tweetB: Nasdaq -0.8% is the number everyone's quoting. The number that matters is that it's an index-level illusion, chips, memory and hardware are eating a much bigger drawdown underneath a headline that looks almost calm.

charts.desc: Text digest, no chart images.

items.title: Morgan Stanley just undercut Coinbase on price for 8.6 million retail crypto customers
items.idea: Morgan Stanley completed its E*TRADE spot crypto rollout (BTC, ETH, SOL via Zerohash) to 8.6 million customers at a 50-basis-point trading fee, undercutting Coinbase, Robinhood and Charles Schwab, which range from 60 to 95 bps. Transfer functionality (moving coins in and out, not just trading) is expected later this year.
items.angle: A bulge-bracket bank entering as a low-cost crypto broker is a structural distribution threat to native crypto exchanges, not just another product launch.
items.tweetA: Morgan Stanley just launched spot crypto to 8.6 million E*TRADE accounts at 50bps, cheaper than Coinbase, Robinhood AND Schwab. A G-SIB bank undercutting native crypto exchanges on price is the kind of distribution shift that shows up in Coinbase's take-rate a year from now, not this week's price action.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching whether BTC ETF flows turn positive. The bigger structural story is a bulge-bracket bank competing on price for retail crypto trading, that's the moment crypto access stops being a differentiator and starts being a commodity feature bundled into a brokerage account.

charts.desc: Text digest, no chart images.

items.title: BTC failed its 50-day EMA again, but the ETF flows just did something they haven't done all month
items.idea: BTC failed to close above its 50-day EMA (~$65,120) and slid to ~$64,087 on the Iran re-escalation and chip slide, but spot BTC ETFs drew $191.1M over two days, reversing a 10-day, $2.73B outflow streak, with IBIT leading at +$81M. Futures open interest rose 3.52% to $48.90B with neutral funding and only $31.66M in short liquidations, describing balanced rather than leveraged positioning. ETH is up 7% over five days vs BTC's 2%, but in the 2022 bear market ETH began outperforming BTC several months before BTC actually bottomed.
items.angle: The historical ETH-leads-BTC parallel is the non-obvious part, it argues for owning ETH now as a recovery signal while staying skeptical BTC has actually bottomed.
items.tweetA: IBIT just led a $191M two-day reversal of a 10-day, $2.73B BTC ETF outflow streak. That's real institutional demand, not a squeeze, OI is up but funding is neutral and short liquidations were tiny. The mechanical bid failed at the 50-day EMA; the organic bid didn't.
items.tweetB: ETH is beating BTC 7% to 2% over five days and everyone's calling it bullish. In 2022, ETH led BTC by several months BEFORE bitcoin actually bottomed. Read correctly, ETH outperformance right now is a recovery-forming signal, not a low-is-in signal, own ETH, stay disciplined on BTC.

charts.desc: Data-dense digest, likely paired with a BTC 50-day EMA chart in the full post. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: The $53B Stripe-PayPal bid is still live and nobody knows if PayPal's board will take it
items.idea: Stripe and Advent International's $60.50/share, above-$53B bid for PayPal remains outstanding with a board decision expected as early as next week, a deal that would unite two of the largest players building out stablecoin infrastructure (PayPal's PYUSD carries a $2.8B market cap).
items.angle: Confirms the deal is still unresolved days later and adds the PYUSD market-cap detail, which sharpens the stablecoin-consolidation read on why Stripe wants this specific target.
items.tweetA: The Stripe-PayPal deal has been 'pending a board decision' for what feels like weeks now. PYUSD's $2.8B market cap is the number that explains why Stripe wants this specific target, not PayPal's checkout business, its stablecoin float.
items.tweetB: If PayPal's board says yes, this becomes the largest fintech acquisition ever, engineered to combine two stablecoin rails under one balance sheet. If they say no, that's arguably the more interesting outcome, it tells you PayPal's board thinks PYUSD is worth more alone.

charts.desc: Text digest, no chart images.

items.title: Gold miners are now the cheapest they've ever been relative to the S&P 500
items.idea: Tavi Costa flags that gold miners are trading at their cheapest level relative to the S&P 500 in recorded history, framing the current move as an 'uncomfortable accumulation phase' where underlying fundamentals remain strong even as prices continue to shake out weak hands.
items.angle: A historic-extreme relative-value claim worth its own tweet even without the full report, and it directly contradicts the Daily News Wall's same-week note that 'gold and silver continue to struggle while crypto has a shining moment.'
items.tweetA: Gold miners vs the S&P 500: cheapest ratio in recorded history, per Tavi Costa. That's not a modest value gap, that's the kind of dislocation that either means miners are structurally broken or this is generational value.
items.tweetB: Same week gold and silver are 'struggling' while crypto has its moment, per one newsletter, and Tavi Costa is calling gold miners historically cheapest vs. the S&P in another. Two sources, opposite reads on precious metals, same 24 hours.

charts.desc: Email references 'the chart below' showing gold miners vs S&P 500 at an all-time relative low. A single ratio chart at a record extreme is exactly the kind that tells a story alone. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom
items.title: Netflix beat on EPS, missed on guidance, and fell to a two-year low, because nobody can name its next growth engine
items.idea: Netflix revenue rose 13% YoY to $12.56B (fractionally below the $12.58B consensus) with EPS of $0.80 beating estimates, but Q3 revenue guidance of $12.86B missed the roughly $13B consensus and full-year guidance was trimmed to $51-51.4B. Viewing hours grew just 2% in H1 2026, and the company will now publish its engagement report annually instead of twice a year.
items.angle: The cut in disclosure frequency is itself a signal, not just the guide, companies stop bragging about metrics right when those metrics stop being flattering.
items.tweetA: Netflix beat EPS and still hit a two-year low. The tell isn't the number, it's that they're cutting engagement disclosure from twice a year to once, that's what a company does when the metric it used to brag about stops being flattering.
items.tweetB: 2% viewing-hours growth in H1 against YouTube, TikTok, live sport and free content. Netflix isn't declining, it's maturing into a media company being priced like a tech company, the multiple compression is the market catching up to that, not a broken business.

charts.desc: No embedded chart; likely a revenue/guidance beat-miss table in the full post. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom (via UBS's Arend Kapteyn)
items.title: China quietly cut oil imports 42% since March, and that's the real reason Hormuz didn't blow up prices
items.idea: UBS economist Arend Kapteyn's data shows Chinese crude and refined-product imports have fallen roughly 42% since March (-25% in April, -15% in May, -10% in June), removing about 5.2 million barrels/day of demand, comparable to the ~3.5 mbpd offset from SPR/IEA emergency releases. Against a theoretical 20.5 mbpd full-Hormuz-closure disruption (reduced to ~14.5 mbpd given continued Saudi/UAE pipeline flows), China's demand retreat is one of the largest single stabilizing factors in the oil market, and Beijing has shown 'little urgency to restock even when shipping conditions briefly improved.'
items.angle: China's restraint is a policy choice, not a structural constraint, meaning the cushion can disappear as fast as it appeared the moment Beijing decides to rebuild inventories.
items.tweetA: China cut oil imports 42% since March, and that quietly did more to keep Hormuz from becoming an oil-price crisis than the IEA's emergency releases did. Nobody's pricing what happens to crude the moment Beijing decides it wants its inventories back.
items.tweetB: 5.2 million barrels/day of Chinese demand voluntarily pulled off the table vs. a 14.5mbpd effective Hormuz shortfall. China isn't cushioning the oil market by accident, it's a lever Beijing is choosing not to pull yet, and that's a very different risk than a real supply gap.

charts.desc: Text piece citing UBS import-volume data series (Feb-June). Charts inside — worth opening for the import-decline chart.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom (via BofA, Deutsche Bank, SocGen, Goldman)
items.title: Equities are 64.7% of a $72.9 trillion fund universe, the highest weighting on record
items.idea: SocGen's Arthur van Slooten notes equities now account for 64.7% of assets across EPFR Global's $72.9T fund universe (ex-commodities), a record. Trend-following CTAs sit in the 72nd percentile of historical equity exposure, vol-control funds in the 91st percentile, BofA's cash levels are at extreme lows and its Bull & Bear indicator is in warning territory, while Goldman notes momentum just suffered its sharpest selloff since the early 2000s even as overall positioning stays elevated.
items.angle: Four independent bank positioning gauges converging on the same 'everyone's already in' conclusion, this is the setup, not yet a trigger.
items.tweetA: 64.7% of a $72.9 trillion global fund universe is sitting in equities right now, a record. When four different banks' positioning gauges (BofA cash levels, CTA exposure, vol-control funds, SocGen allocation) all say the same thing in the same week, that's not noise, that's the setup.
items.tweetB: Vol-control funds are in the 91st percentile of historical equity exposure. These are mechanical sellers when volatility rises, not discretionary ones. The bull case here isn't wrong, it's just fully priced by definition, which is a different risk than being wrong.

charts.desc: Text-heavy positioning roundup citing multiple bank surveys. Charts inside — worth opening for the BofA Bull & Bear indicator chart.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side Of The Boom (via Bloomberg's Simon White)
items.title: Leveraged ETF demand is fading, but hedge funds are quietly taking the leverage baton
items.idea: Bloomberg's Simon White shows banks' equity repo books (the funding source for hedge fund and leveraged-ETF gearing) continuing to climb even as leveraged equity ETF market cap softens and weekly net inflows fade toward zero, because hedge fund long/short equity exposure sensitivity to the S&P 500 has been rising steadily all year, picking up where ETF demand is leaving off. White flags swap spreads as the early-warning gauge: they fall first when bank balance sheets get congested, before financing actually tightens.
items.angle: The composition of leverage matters, hedge funds unwind faster and less predictably than retail ETF flows once financing tightens.
items.tweetA: Leveraged ETF demand is fading. Equity repo is still climbing. That gap means hedge funds have quietly become the marginal source of equity leverage in this market, and hedge funds don't deleverage gradually, they get margin-called and dump into whatever's bidding.
items.tweetB: Watch swap spreads, not VIX, for the actual warning sign here. Simon White's framework: swap spreads fall first when bank balance sheets get congested, before financing actually tightens and forces hedge funds to cut leveraged equity exposure into a falling market.

charts.desc: Text piece citing a Bloomberg data series on equity repo vs leveraged ETF market cap. Charts inside — worth opening for the repo-vs-ETF divergence chart.
